cards.text: To use std::string_view, include the header {{c1::<string_view>}} (C++17).
std::string_view provides a {{c1::read-only}} view of a string without making a copy.
Best practice for function parameters: use {{c1::std::string_view}} instead of {{c2::const std::string&}} for read-only string parameters.
std::string_view is {{c1::cheap to copy}} because it only stores a pointer and length, not the actual string data.

cards.question: What's more efficient and why?
cards.answer: Option 2 is more efficient. string_view can accept both C-style strings and std::string without conversion, while const std::string& may require creating a temporary std::string object.
cards.explanation: string_view provides a lightweight view without copying or conversion overhead.

cards.question: What does this code demonstrate?
cards.answer: string_view can accept both std::string and C-style string literals without conversion.
cards.explanation: This flexibility makes string_view ideal for function parameters that need to accept different string types.

cards.front: What are the two main benefits of std::string_view over const std::string&?
cards.back:
• No copies or conversions when passing C-style strings
  • Cheap to copy (pass by value) - just pointer + length

cards.front: Can you modify a string through a std::string_view?
cards.back: No, std::string_view is read-only. It provides a view of the string but doesn't allow modification.

cards.explanation:
Use std::string_view for:
  • Function parameters (read-only strings)
  • Avoiding unnecessary copies
  • Working with both std::string and C-strings
Don't use for:
  • Storing strings (lifetime issues)
  • Functions that need to modify strings
  • Return values (usually)
examples: void process(std::string_view sv);  // Good for parameters

cards.back: Lightweight read-only view of a string that doesn't own the data (C++17)
